channel.title: Surfaced — Discover What's Next
channel.description: Fresh discoveries, trending products, hidden gems, future tech, and daily tools — curated every day.

item.title: GitHub CLI telemetry
item.description: This page details GitHub CLI's pseudoanonymous telemetry collection. It explains that the GitHub Command Line Interface (CLI) now gathers opt-in, anonymized data about how users interact with the tool. This telemetry includes information like command usage, errors encountered, and system configuration. The purpose is to help the GitHub team understand user behavior, identify areas for improvement, and prioritize feature development for the CLI. Users can review the data collected and manage their participation.

item.title: GoModel
item.description: GoModel is an open-source AI gateway built in Go, designed to simplify the integration and management of various AI models. It acts as a unified interface for different AI services and models, allowing developers to interact with them through a consistent API. This means you can switch between different AI providers or models without rewriting significant parts of your application. It's particularly useful for backend developers building applications that rely on machine learning capabilities, aiming to reduce complexity and accelerate development.

item.title: Windows 9x Subsystem for Linux
item.description: This project aims to bring the functionality of a Linux subsystem to the classic Windows 9x operating system. It allows users to run Linux command-line tools and potentially even graphical applications within a Windows 95/98 environment. The goal is to bridge the gap between legacy Windows systems and modern open-source software, enabling users to access a wider range of utilities and development tools on older hardware. It's particularly interesting for retro computing enthusiasts and those exploring historical operating system capabilities.

item.title: VidStudio
item.description: VidStudio is a powerful, browser-based video editor designed for users who want to create and edit videos without the hassle of uploading large files. It leverages WebAssembly and client-side processing to offer a smooth editing experience directly within your web browser. This means your raw footage stays on your device, enhancing privacy and speed for edits. It's ideal for quick social media video cuts, simple tutorial creation, or trimming existing footage without requiring desktop software.

item.title: John Ternus to become Apple CEO
item.description: This news article, originating from Apple's official newsroom, announces a significant leadership transition within Apple Inc. It details that John Ternus is set to assume the role of Chief Executive Officer, while Tim Cook will move to the position of Executive Chairman. The announcement outlines the effective date of this change and highlights the company's forward-looking strategy under new leadership.

item.title: Laws of Software Engineering
item.description: The Laws of Software Engineering is a curated collection of fundamental principles and best practices that govern the design, development, and maintenance of software systems. It compiles widely accepted axioms and heuristics, presented in a clear and accessible manner, to help engineers build more robust, scalable, and maintainable software. The site serves as a central repository for critical software engineering wisdom.

item.title: MacMind
item.description: MacMind is a fascinating project that recreates a functional HyperCard environment within a modern web browser, powered by a transformer neural network. It allows users to interact with a simulated 1989 Macintosh experience, complete with the iconic HyperCard interface and its stack-based organization. This tool is built by SeanFDZ and offers a unique way to explore and understand the early days of personal computing and interactive multimedia.

item.title: Arctic Permafrost Releases Ancient Viruses, Potent Threat
item.description: In 2024, a team of French and Russian scientists, led by Dr. Jean-Michel Claverie of Aix-Marseille University, successfully revived several strains of ancient viruses that had been frozen in Siberian permafrost for tens of thousands of years. The oldest revived virus, a strain of *Pandoravirus* dating back 48,500 years, was isolated from a sample of permafrost taken from the deepest layer ever studied. This research, published in *Viruses*, highlights the potential for these 'zombie viruses' to pose a significant, albeit currently theoretical, threat to public health as global temperatures rise and thaw these ancient ice reserves.

item.title: EU to Force Replaceable Batteries in Phones and Tablets from 2027
item.description: This is an article reporting on a new EU regulation that will require all smartphones and tablets sold within the European Union to have batteries that are easily replaceable by consumers or independent repairers. This directive, set to take effect in 2027, aims to combat e-waste and promote device longevity by moving away from sealed-unit designs. It's a significant policy shift with potential global implications for consumer electronics manufacturing and repairability.

item.title: Ancient Chinese Musical Instrument Reveals Complex Notation
item.description: The discovery of a nearly intact 2,400-year-old *se* zither in a tomb in Hubei Province, China, has provided archaeologists and musicologists with an extraordinary glimpse into ancient Chinese musical practices. Unearthed in 2020, the instrument, along with its original strings and protective casing, was accompanied by a detailed musical score inscribed onto bamboo slips. This score, analyzed by researchers including Dr. Li Yu of the Institute of Archaeology, Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, reveals a sophisticated system of musical notation far more advanced than previously assumed for the period.

item.title: GitHub's Fake Star Economy
item.description: This investigative piece delves into the phenomenon of fake stars on GitHub, exploring how metrics like repository stars can be artificially inflated. It examines the mechanisms and motivations behind this practice, which can distort the perceived popularity and quality of open-source projects. The article is crucial for developers, project maintainers, and users who rely on GitHub's metrics for evaluating software and contributions.

item.title: Artificial Wormholes Created in Lab Testing Einstein Theory
item.description: Physicists at the University of Maryland, led by Dr. Christopher Monroe and his team, have successfully created and manipulated what they describe as a 'traversable artificial wormhole' in a lab setting. Published in *Nature Physics* in 2022, this groundbreaking experiment used entangled quantum bits (qubits) to simulate the connection between two points in spacetime, a phenomenon theorized by Einstein but never before observed. While not a physical tunnel through space, it replicates the mathematical properties of a wormhole, allowing information to 'travel' between seemingly disconnected quantum states.

item.title: All phones sold in the EU to have replaceable batteries from 2027
item.description: This news article reports on a significant legislative change by the European Union mandating that all phones and tablets sold within the EU must feature easily replaceable batteries starting in 2027. The initiative aims to reduce electronic waste and extend the lifespan of devices by empowering consumers to perform common repairs themselves. This policy is a response to growing environmental concerns and the trend of sealed, difficult-to-repair electronics.

item.title: Ancient Bird Fossil Reveals Unexpected Flight Mechanism
item.description: A remarkably preserved fossil of *Asteriornis maastrichtensis*, a 66-million-year-old bird discovered in Belgium, has provided unprecedented insights into the evolutionary transition from non-avian dinosaurs to modern birds. Published in *Nature* in 2022 by a team led by Albert Chen and Daniel Field from the University of Cambridge, the fossil's detailed skull anatomy suggests it possessed a 'modern' jaw mechanism that allowed for more agile feeding and potentially different flight adaptations than previously thought for birds of this era, just before the K-Pg extinction event.

item.title: Ithihāsas – a character explorer for Hindu epics
item.description: Ithihāsas is a web application designed to explore and understand the complex characters and relationships within Hindu epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata. Built in a remarkably short time, it provides an interactive interface to navigate through characters, their lineage, key events, and connections. This tool is perfect for students, scholars, or anyone curious about the rich narratives and intricate character webs of these ancient texts.

item.title: Earth's Core Rotation Slowing: Implications for Day Length
item.description: Researchers Yi Yang and Xiaodong Song at Peking University have presented compelling evidence suggesting that the Earth's inner core's rotation might have significantly slowed down, or even paused, around 2009-2010, and may now be rotating in the opposite direction relative to the mantle. Analyzing seismic waves from repeating earthquakes over several decades, they observed distinct changes in wave travel times, pointing to a slowdown and potential reversal. This phenomenon could subtly influence the length of our days.

item.title: TRELLIS.2 Image-to-3D generation
item.description: TRELLIS.2 is a powerful open-source tool that enables users to generate 3D models directly from 2D images, natively on Apple Silicon hardware. Developed by Shivam P. Kumar, it leverages advanced diffusion models to create detailed and accurate 3D representations. This is ideal for artists, game developers, or hobbyists looking to quickly prototype 3D assets from existing imagery without complex manual modeling processes.

item.title: Ancient Chinese Silk Dyeing Secrets
item.description: A team of materials scientists and archaeologists, led by Professor Peiyuan Xu from Peking University, has successfully replicated ancient Chinese silk dyeing techniques from the Tang Dynasty (618–907 CE), as detailed in 'Nature Communications' in 2023. Using historical texts and chemical analysis of surviving silk fragments, they rediscovered the complex multi-step processes and specific plant-based mordants used to achieve the vibrant, colorfast hues seen in surviving artifacts. This breakthrough allows for the conservation and recreation of historically significant textiles.

item.title: Pixel Perfect Editor
item.description: Pixel Perfect Editor is a lightweight, web-based image editing tool focused on precise pixel manipulation and color adjustments. It allows users to zoom in to the pixel level, make intricate edits, and apply color grading with fine-grained control, all within the browser. It's designed for designers and artists who need exactitude in their image work, offering a streamlined interface that avoids the complexity of full-fledged desktop software. Users can import images, make detailed adjustments, and export their modified creations.

item.title: Deep-Sea Corals Thrive on Volcanic Heat
item.description: A groundbreaking study published in 'Frontiers in Marine Science' in 2022 by researchers from the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution revealed that certain deep-sea coral species are heavily reliant on hydrothermal vents for their survival. Analyzing a unique community near the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, they found that the corals not only tolerate the extreme temperatures and chemical compositions but actively colonize areas warmed by volcanic activity. This challenges the perception of deep-sea corals as solely dependent on ambient cold ocean currents.

item.title: Fathom 3.0
item.description: Fathom 3.0 is an advanced AI-powered meeting notetaking tool designed to streamline your workflow. This latest version eliminates the need for bots, offering direct integration with popular AI models like ChatGPT and Claude. It provides real-time summaries during meetings and account-wide AI search for insights, making it ideal for professionals who attend numerous virtual or in-person discussions.

item.title: Opus Token Leaderboard
item.description: The Opus Token Leaderboard is a website that anonymously compares the request-token performance of Opus 4.6 and Opus 4.7, two versions of an AI model. It presents data on how these models perform on various tasks, focusing on their token efficiency and output quality. The comparisons are conducted anonymously, providing an objective benchmark for developers and users evaluating different AI model versions. This tool helps users understand which AI model might be more suitable for their specific needs based on empirical performance data.

item.title: Early Hominin Tool Use in Africa
item.description: Paleoanthropologists unearthed stone tools and hominin fossil fragments in Ethiopia, dated to approximately 2.6 million years ago, as reported in 'Science' in 2021. The site, known as Bokol Dora 1, revealed remarkably preserved Oldowan tools alongside a mandible fragment. This finding provides some of the earliest definitive evidence of hominin stone tool manufacture and use in East Africa, predating previously accepted sites.

item.title: Jupid
item.description: Jupid is an innovative financial technology platform designed to bridge the gap between powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) and the complexities of financial transactions. It addresses the common issues of context loss, inconsistent categorization, and lack of memory that plague LLMs when handling financial data. By connecting directly to your bank accounts, Jupid aims to provide a reliable data layer for more accurate and efficient financial management.

item.title: NIST Scientists Create 'Any Wavelength' Lasers
item.description: This news article from NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) details a breakthrough by their scientists in creating lasers capable of emitting light at virtually any wavelength. This advancement utilizes tiny circuits to manipulate light, offering unprecedented flexibility in laser technology. The development has significant implications for various fields, including telecommunications, sensing, and scientific research, by enabling custom-tailored light sources. This is a scientific discovery rather than a direct tool for end-users, but it highlights groundbreaking technological progress.

item.title: Ancient Mesopotamian Algebra Uncovered
item.description: A team led by Dr. Eleanor Robson of the University of Cambridge deciphered a cuneiform tablet from ancient Mesopotamia, dating back to around 1800 BCE, revealing sophisticated algebraic techniques. Published in 'Historia Mathematica' in 2022, the tablet demonstrates methods for solving quadratic equations far earlier than previously thought. This finding rewrites the history of mathematics, pushing back the timeline for abstract problem-solving.

item.title: Google Pixel 8a
item.description: The Google Pixel 8a is the latest affordable smartphone from Google, packing flagship-level AI capabilities into a budget-friendly device. It features Google's powerful Tensor G3 chip, a high-refresh-rate OLED display, and an advanced camera system enhanced by Google's computational photography. This phone is designed for users who want a premium smartphone experience, including cutting-edge AI features and a superior camera, without the premium price tag.

item.title: Track Succession
item.description: Track Succession provides real-time, publicly accessible data on every CEO and CFO change at US public companies, sourced directly from SEC filings. The platform aims to offer transparency into corporate leadership transitions, allowing users to track historical and ongoing executive shifts. It's a valuable resource for investors, researchers, and anyone interested in the dynamics of corporate governance. The 'explore' section allows for detailed filtering and analysis of this data.

item.title: Vast Antarctic Fungus Network Found
item.description: Researchers have discovered a colossal underground fungal network spanning hundreds of square kilometers in Antarctica, identified through extensive soil sampling and DNA analysis. Published in 'Nature Ecology & Evolution' in 2023, the findings suggest this ancient mycelial web is a crucial but previously unrecognized component of the continent's sparse ecosystem. This discovery challenges our understanding of subterranean life and nutrient cycling in extreme environments.

item.title: Anker 737 Power Bank (PowerCore 24K Hi-Fi)
item.description: The Anker 737 Power Bank is a high-capacity portable charger designed for power users and travelers. With a 24,000mAh capacity and 140W total output, it can simultaneously charge multiple devices, including laptops, tablets, and smartphones, at rapid speeds. Its standout feature is the smart digital display that shows power output, input, and estimated recharge time, offering unprecedented transparency into its charging status.

item.title: Oberon System 3 Native
item.description: Oberon System 3 is a compact and efficient operating system and programming environment designed for simplicity and performance. This specific release allows the full Oberon System 3 experience to run natively on a Raspberry Pi 3, eliminating the need for emulation or complex setup. It's developed by a community of enthusiasts aiming to revive and modernize this powerful, yet often overlooked, computing platform. Users can boot directly into a graphical interface and begin coding or exploring the system's capabilities immediately.

item.title: Underwater Maya City Complex Revealed
item.description: In 2023, archaeologists announced the discovery of a vast, previously unknown Maya city complex submerged off the coast of Belize, dating back to the Late Classic period (approximately 600-900 CE). Using advanced sonar and underwater surveying techniques, researchers from the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution identified numerous structures, including pyramids, plazas, and residential buildings, preserved beneath the waves. This astonishing find offers new insights into Maya coastal settlements and their interactions with the marine environment.

item.title: Logitech MX Master 4 Mouse
item.description: The Logitech MX Master 4 is a wireless, ergonomic mouse designed for ultimate productivity and comfort. It features advanced tracking on virtually any surface, a hyper-fast scroll wheel for navigating long documents or code, and customizable buttons that can be programmed for specific application shortcuts. This mouse is ideal for professionals, creatives, and power users who spend long hours at their computer and demand precision and efficiency.

item.title: Claude Design
item.description: Claude Design is an experimental initiative by Anthropic that explores the potential of large language models, like Claude, in the creative process of design. It showcases how AI can be used as a collaborative partner for generating design concepts, iterating on ideas, and even producing visual elements. This tool is aimed at designers, artists, and anyone interested in the intersection of AI and creativity. A product designer could use Claude Design to brainstorm initial interface layouts for a new app, providing prompts to generate various visual directions before committing to a specific path.

item.title: Lunar Rock Samples Show Water Formed Very Early
item.description: Analysis of lunar rock samples brought back by China's Chang'e-5 mission, published in *Nature Geoscience* in 2021, has provided compelling evidence that water existed on the Moon much earlier than previously thought. Researchers, including Dr. Huicun He from the Institute of Geology and Geophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, found hydroxyl (OH) embedded within mineral grains, indicating that water-forming processes were active on the Moon as far back as 2 billion years ago, potentially even earlier. This discovery has profound implications for our understanding of the early solar system and the evolution of celestial bodies.

item.title: Velo
item.description: Velo is a new tool that transforms raw screen recordings into polished, shareable video messages. Leveraging AI, it simplifies the process of creating professional-looking videos from your computer screen. This is perfect for anyone who needs to communicate complex ideas visually, provide software walkthroughs, or offer feedback in a dynamic and engaging format, moving beyond static screenshots or lengthy text explanations.

item.title: PanicLock
item.description: PanicLock is a macOS utility that enhances security by disabling Touch ID and requiring a full password unlock when your MacBook's lid is closed. This prevents unauthorized access if your device is stolen or accessed without your knowledge while you're away from your desk. It's particularly useful for individuals working with sensitive data who need an extra layer of protection beyond standard lock screen settings. A remote worker at a coffee shop could use PanicLock to ensure that even if their laptop is briefly unattended, it cannot be woken and accessed via fingerprint.

item.title: Deep-Sea Microbes Found to Eat Plastic Bottles
item.description: In a groundbreaking discovery published in the journal *Frontiers in Marine Science* in 2021, researchers from the Ocean University of China identified a novel consortium of deep-sea microbes capable of degrading polyethylene terephthalate (PET), the plastic commonly used in beverage bottles. Lead scientist Dr. Jianhua Li noted that these microorganisms, isolated from sediment samples collected at a depth of 2,500 meters in the South China Sea, possess unique enzymes that can break down PET into simpler, non-toxic compounds. This finding opens up significant avenues for tackling global plastic pollution.

item.title: Agentplace AI Agents
item.description: Agentplace offers a platform for creating and deploying specialized AI agents designed for specific real-world tasks and workflows. Users can start with pre-built agents for common needs or customize their own within minutes. These agents can handle everything from lead routing and in-depth research to document analysis and appointment scheduling, making them ideal for businesses and professionals looking to automate repetitive and complex processes.

item.title: Interval Calculator
item.description: This is a specialized calculator designed to perform operations on disjoint sets of intervals. It allows users to input multiple, non-overlapping ranges and then perform calculations such as union, intersection, difference, and more, outputting the resulting set of intervals. It's built by Victor Poughon and is useful for anyone working with mathematical or computational problems involving ranges. A researcher analyzing data that is only valid within specific time windows could use this calculator to efficiently find the combined valid periods after several distinct data collection phases.

item.title: GitHub CLI Telemetry Collection
item.description: The GitHub Command Line Interface (CLI) now collects pseudo-anonymous telemetry data to understand user behavior and identify areas for improvement. This means that while usage data is gathered, it's designed to protect individual user identity and privacy. The breakthrough lies in implementing a privacy-preserving telemetry system that provides valuable insights into tool usage patterns without compromising user anonymity.

item.title: Ancient Tree Fossil Found Under Antarctic Ice
item.description: A team of paleontologists, led by Dr. Stephen Jones of the British Antarctic Survey, announced in 2023 the discovery of a remarkably well-preserved fossilized tree, estimated to be around 260 million years old, found deep beneath the Antarctic ice sheet. The specimen, a cast of a Glossopteris tree, was unearthed during an ice core drilling expedition in the Transantarctic Mountains. Its existence provides compelling evidence that Antarctica was once a temperate region capable of supporting lush forests, drastically altering our understanding of Earth's past climate and continental drift.

item.title: ProdShort
item.description: ProdShort transforms spoken content from your meetings into ready-to-post social media shorts and posts, addressing the challenge of creating authentic and time-consuming content. Unlike AI content generators that can sound fake, ProdShort captures the genuine value of your conversations, making them easily shareable. This is an ideal solution for founders, marketers, and teams looking to efficiently leverage their internal discussions for external communication and content marketing.

item.title: Smol machines
item.description: Smol machines are incredibly small, portable virtual machines designed for sub-second coldstarts. They allow you to package and run applications with their dependencies in a lightweight, isolated environment, ensuring consistency across different systems. This is particularly beneficial for developers needing to test or deploy applications quickly and reliably. For instance, a developer could package a web server application with its specific runtime and libraries into a smol machine to guarantee it runs identically in development, staging, and production.

item.title: 3.4 Million Solar Panels
item.description: This milestone highlights the deployment of an enormous solar farm comprising 3.4 million individual solar panels, representing a significant step in large-scale renewable energy generation. The project, spearheaded by American Solar Farms, aims to harness solar power at an industrial level, contributing substantially to the clean energy grid. It leverages advancements in photovoltaic technology and efficient land utilization for maximum energy capture.

item.title: Bronze Age City Used Advanced Flood Control Systems
item.description: Archaeologists have unearthed evidence of a sophisticated Bronze Age city in China's Yellow River valley, dating back approximately 4,000 years, featuring remarkably advanced water management and flood control systems. The discovery, detailed in studies by researchers from the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, includes a complex network of dikes, channels, and reservoirs designed to protect the settlement from devastating floods. This finding challenges previous assumptions about the technological capabilities of early Chinese civilizations and their understanding of hydraulic engineering.

item.title: Littlebird
item.description: Littlebird is an AI assistant designed to understand and integrate with your existing work context, providing highly relevant assistance. It gains insight by observing your screen activity and transcribing meetings, allowing it to generate more accurate answers, drafts, and plans. This makes Littlebird a powerful tool for professionals who need an AI assistant that truly grasps their ongoing projects and workflow, enhancing productivity and reducing the need for constant context re-explanation.

item.title: boringBar
item.description: boringBar is a lightweight, macOS-native taskbar-style dock replacement that aims to simplify your workflow by presenting open applications and essential system information in a clean, unobtrusive bar at the top of your screen. It's designed for users who find the default macOS Dock distracting or prefer a more minimalist interface. For example, a graphic designer could use boringBar to keep track of their open Adobe Suite applications and quickly switch between them without cluttering their main desktop space.

item.title: Making RAM at Home
item.description: This refers to a project that demonstrates the feasibility of constructing basic Random Access Memory (RAM) modules using accessible components and a home laboratory setup. The video showcases a step-by-step process, likely involving discrete electronic components like transistors and capacitors, to build functional memory circuits. The achievement is in demystifying and enabling DIY creation of fundamental computing hardware, even if at a rudimentary level.

item.title: Ancient DNA Reveals Surprising European Migrations
item.description: Studies published in journals like Nature in 2018 and 2022, led by researchers such as David Reich at Harvard Medical School and Eske Willerslev at the University of Cambridge, have utilized ancient DNA to reconstruct complex human migration patterns across prehistoric Europe. These findings have revealed significant population turnovers and genetic admixtures, overturning previous assumptions about the stability of early European societies. For instance, it was discovered that the spread of farming from the Near East involved a massive influx of people, and later, the Yamnaya culture from the Pontic-Caspian steppe dramatically reshaped the genetic landscape of Europe.
